prior.content_md:
**Plain-language summary:** CII measures how efficiently a vessel uses fuel relative to the cargo it moves over the distance it travels, expressed in grams of CO₂ per tonne-nautical mile. Every ship of 5,000 gross tonnage and above must calculate its attained annual operational CII at the end of each calendar year and have it rated A through E by the flag state administration. A is best; E is worst. A ship rated D for three consecutive years, or E for any single year, must submit a corrective action plan to its flag state demonstrating how it will return to a C rating or above.

Rating thresholds are not static: IMO has established annual reduction factors requiring continuous improvement. This means a vessel that was rated C in 2023 on unchanged operations may be rated D or E in subsequent years as the required threshold tightens.

*Source: IMO Press Briefing, "Rules on Ship Carbon Intensity and Rating System Enter into Force," IMO, 1 November 2022. https://www.imo.org/en/MediaCentre/PressBriefings/pages/CII-and-EEXI-entry-into-force.aspx*

*Source: Resolution MEPC.338(76), "2021 Guidelines on the Operational Carbon Intensity Reduction Factors Relative to Reference Lines (CII Reduction Factors Guidelines, G3)," IMO. https://wwwcdn.imo.org/localresources/en/KnowledgeCentre/IndexofIMOResolutions/MEPCDocuments/MEPC.338(76).pdf*

**Why it applies to the workspace:** The workspace, as a freight forwarder, does not own or operate ocean vessels and therefore is not the direct regulated entity under CII. However, every ocean shipment the workspace arranges — whether for fine art transiting Americas-Europe, high-value automotive moving intra-Asia, humanitarian cargo on Asia-Americas lanes, or live event equipment on any trade lane — is carried aboard vessels that are subject to CII. The workspace's ocean product quality, transit time reliability, available capacity, and ability to satisfy client sustainability requirements are all downstream consequences of the CII ratings held by the carriers whose space the workspace books.

Ocean is the workspace's tertiary transport mode. However, for cargo verticals such as humanitarian and high-value automotive — where ocean consolidation or dedicated ocean services are operationally relevant — vessel availability and carrier behaviour under CII are material procurement and client-commitment risks.
